1: Thus says YHWH: Maintain justice and do what is right, for my salvation is coming soon and my righteousness is about to be revealed. The ethical imperative precedes the eschatological promise: keeping mishpat and doing righteousness are the conditions of the covenant community's life, not prerequisites for earning the salvation. The salvation is already coming; the ethical life is the appropriate response to and participation in the approaching divine act.
2: Blessed is the man who does this and the person who holds to it — who keeps the sabbath without profaning it and refrains from doing any evil. Sabbath-keeping as the visible covenantal marker was the distinctive practice of the exilic and post-exilic community. In Babylon, where the temple calendar was impossible to maintain, the sabbath became the portable covenant sign — the one practice that identified Israel as YHWH's covenant people without requiring land, temple, or king.
3: Let not the foreigner who has joined himself to YHWH say, 'The LORD will surely exclude me from his people'; and let not the eunuch say, 'I am a withered tree.' The two excluded categories of Deuteronomy 23:1, 3-8 — eunuchs (Isaiah 56:1) and certain foreigners (Ammonites/Moabites, Isaiah 56:3-8) — are explicitly addressed. The foreigner's fear of exclusion and the eunuch's self-identification as a withered tree (no biological legacy) represent the shame of the marginalized. The oracle overturns both exclusions.
4: To the eunuchs who keep my sabbaths, who choose what pleases me and hold fast to my covenant — the criteria for inclusion shift entirely from biological status to ethical and covenantal practice: sabbath-keeping, choosing YHWH's will, holding the covenant. Eunuchs in ANE royal courts (Babylon, Persia, Assyria) were often powerful administrators and confidants of the king; the Daniel narrative operates in this environment. Their exclusion from the covenant assembly despite their importance in imperial service is addressed here.
5: I will give them within my house and within my walls a memorial and a name (yad va-shem, literally hand and name) better than sons and daughters — an everlasting name that will never be cut off. The yad va-shem (hand and name, the phrase from which Yad Vashem draws its name) provides the eunuch with permanent covenant memorial in place of biological legacy. Sons and daughters were the normal vehicle for name-perpetuation in the ANE; the eunuch who cannot have biological children receives a name that outlasts all biological lineages.
6: The foreigners who join themselves to YHWH, to serve him, to love the name of YHWH, and to be his servants — everyone who keeps the sabbath without profaning it and holds fast my covenant. The three criteria for the foreigner's full inclusion — service, love of the name, sabbath-keeping — are identical to what is asked of the native Israelite. The covenant community is redefined around practice and commitment rather than genealogy, anticipating the full Gentile inclusion of the New Testament.
7: Even them I will bring to my holy mountain and make them glad in my house of prayer; their burnt offerings and sacrifices will be accepted on my altar — for my house shall be called a house of prayer for all peoples. The temple's purpose as a house of prayer for all peoples is the most universalist statement in the Hebrew Bible's temple theology. The temple was constructed with zones of exclusion (Court of Women, Court of Israel, Court of Priests) that kept non-Israelites at a distance. Jesus quotes this verse (Mark 11:17; Matthew 21:13) when cleansing the temple — invoking its original intention against its corrupted present practice.
8: The Lord YHWH, who gathers the outcasts of Israel, declares: I will gather yet others to him besides those already gathered to him. The progressive ingathering: the restoration of the scattered Israelites is not the endpoint but the penultimate stage. Beyond the gathered Israelites, YHWH will gather yet more — the foreigner and eunuch of Isaiah 56:3-6 are the initial examples, but the others gestures toward a gathering that exceeds ethnic Israel. This is the open horizon of Second Isaiah's universalism.
9: All you wild animals of the field, come to devour — all you beasts of the forest. The sudden shift to an invitation to predators signals a judgment oracle against Israel's failed leaders (Isaiah 56:10-12). In ANE treaty and prophetic literature, wild animals devouring a flock signaled the complete failure of shepherds to protect their charges. The invitation to the beasts is ironic: since the watchmen have failed, the natural predators may as well come.
10: Israel's watchmen are all blind; none of them understand. They are all silent dogs that cannot bark — they dream, lie down, and love to sleep. The watchmen on city walls were the first line of warning against approaching enemies; the dogs patrolling at night barked at intruders. Both failures are catastrophic: blind watchmen and mute dogs leave the community undefended. The critique targets the prophetic and priestly leadership of the exile period — those who should have warned, interpreted events, and led the community's response have instead retreated into passivity.
11: The dogs are ravenous — never satisfied; and the shepherds have no understanding; they have all gone their own way, each after his own profit. The ravenous-dog image intensifies the critique: not merely lazy but actively self-seeking. The shepherd-for-profit trope is Ezekiel 34's theme throughout — the shepherds who feed themselves rather than the flock. The combination of pastoral failure (no understanding) and economic corruption (pursuing profit) describes a leadership class that has inverted its covenant obligations.
12: Come, they say, let us get wine; let us fill ourselves with strong drink — and tomorrow will be like today, even more abundant. The carpe diem materialism of the corrupt leaders — drinking today, confident tomorrow will bring more — is the ancient world's version of fatalistic hedonism. The phrase tomorrow will be like today, even more abundant captures an attitude of complacency that dismisses prophetic warning about coming judgment. Isaiah 22:13 uses similar language for Jerusalem's pre-siege revelry.
